steps.title: Spot the shared problem
steps.content:
Both texts identify an identical issue: current scholarship fails to fully represent modernist fiction's stylistic landscape.

Text 1 demonstrates this through overrepresenting experimental narrative structures beyond their actual historical influence and underrepresenting coherent narratives like Reed's.

Text 2 demonstrates this through scholars missing stylistic elements that computer analysis could reveal—elements overlooked because of excessive focus on the fragmentation-versus-coherence debate.

steps.title: Find the choice that captures this
steps.content:
The correct response directly states what both authors would agree on: that scholarship only partially captures the stylistic dimensions of the period's fiction.

Text 1 proves this through imbalanced representation—overweighting fragmentation, neglecting coherence—and Text 2 proves it through missing stylistic elements, both revealing an incomplete picture.

The other choices go awry:
  • B claims unsupported method obsolescence
  • C misreads Text 2 as concerned with reputation rather than analytical scope
  • D contradicts Text 1 by suggesting scholars look beyond style


That confirms A is correct.
